name: Feed Sans Narrow
classification_notes: A narrow grotesque sans-serif, typical of condensed grotesque cuts designed for space-efficient editorial and display applications.
summary: A narrow grotesque cut from Feedtype, built for tight measures in editorial and display contexts.
description: Feed Sans Narrow is a space-efficient grotesque designed to perform where column widths are restricted or display settings demand tight setting. The narrow proportion suits headline and UI work where a standard-width grotesque would feel too open. The weight range from Thin to Black gives enough contrast for most typographic hierarchies.
foundry_blurb: Feed Sans Narrow is a condensed grotesque typeface from Feedtype, offering a range of weights suited to editorial and display use where space is at a premium.

name: Feed Sans Condensed
classification_notes: A condensed grotesque sans-serif, typical of the condensed grotesque tradition used in editorial and display contexts where narrow column widths are required.
summary: A condensed grotesque sans designed for tight measures and display use across editorial and identity applications.
description: Feed Sans Condensed is a narrow grotesque from Canadian foundry Feedtype, built for settings where space is at a premium without sacrificing legibility. The family spans a broad weight range suited to both text and headline use. Its restrained character sits comfortably in editorial, signage and branding contexts.
foundry_blurb: Feedtype present Feed Sans Condensed as a condensed grotesque typeface designed for space-efficient typesetting across a wide range of applications.

name: Feed Sans
classification_notes: Feed Sans is a grotesque sans-serif with rational, neutral characteristics typical of the genre, suited to text and display use across a range of applications.
summary: A neutral grotesque sans from Canadian foundry Feedtype, offered across multiple weights for text and display use.
description: Feed Sans is a grotesque sans-serif designed for versatility across both text and display settings. The family spans a range of weights with matching italics, giving it enough range for editorial and identity work. Feedtype is a Canadian type foundry, and Feed Sans sits as a workhorse offering in their catalogue.
foundry_blurb: Feed Sans is a grotesque sans-serif typeface from Feedtype, designed to perform across a variety of typographic contexts from text to display use.

name: Citerne
classification_notes: Citerne appears to be a display-oriented sans-serif with a distinctive, expressive character suited to headline and branding use rather than extended text setting.
summary: A display sans from Canadian foundry Feedtype, designed for headline and identity work.
description: Citerne is a display sans-serif from Feedtype, a Canadian type foundry. The face is oriented towards headline, poster and branding applications where a distinctive sans character is required. Details beyond the basic family structure were not available from the page content provided.
foundry_blurb: Citerne is offered by Feedtype as part of their type catalogue, available through their Canadian foundry website.

name: Youth Grotesque
classification_notes: Named explicitly as a grotesque, Youth Grotesque follows the conventions of the genre with a functional, upright structure typical of European grotesque sans-serifs.
summary: A straightforward grotesque sans from Canadian foundry Feedtype, built for editorial and screen use.
description: Youth Grotesque is a clean grotesque sans from Montreal-based Feedtype, offering four weights from Regular to Black. The design sits within the European grotesque tradition, prioritising legibility and neutral character over personality. Suited to editorial layouts, interface design and branding that calls for an unfussy sans.
foundry_blurb: Feedtype present Youth Grotesque as a grotesque typeface designed for contemporary use, with a range of weights intended to support versatile typographic applications.

name: E8888-001
classification_notes: A highly unconventional display typeface with an experimental, constructed character structure that resists easy classification within traditional categories; its mechanical and abstract letterforms place it firmly in the experimental display space.
summary: An experimental display typeface from Canadian foundry Feedtype with highly abstract, constructed letterforms that sit outside conventional typographic traditions.
description: Built on an unconventional structural logic, E8888-001 pushes letterform construction toward abstract, mechanical territory rarely seen in functional type. The foundry Feedtype, based in Canada, produces work oriented toward the experimental end of type design, and this release reflects that position. It suits editorial, poster and identity contexts where typographic distinctiveness takes priority over legibility at small sizes.
foundry_blurb: Feedtype present E8888-001 as a typeface that challenges conventional letterform construction, reflecting the foundry's interest in experimental and avant-garde type design.

name: Guillon
classification_notes: Guillon presents the characteristics of a transitional serif, with moderate contrast between thick and thin strokes and bracketed serifs suited to text and editorial work.
summary: A transitional serif from Canadian foundry Feedtype, designed for editorial and text-setting applications.
description: Guillon is a transitional serif with bracketed serifs and moderate stroke contrast, offering a range of weights suited to both body text and display use. Released by Feedtype, the Montreal-based foundry, it carries a quiet formality balanced by approachable proportions. The family includes italics across its weight range, making it adaptable for long-form editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Feedtype present Guillon as part of their typeface offering, available through their Canadian type foundry.

name: Hochelaga
classification_notes: The page presents Hochelaga as a display typeface with strong serif characteristics designed for headline and expressive use, placing it firmly in the display serif subcategory.
summary: A display serif from Montreal-based Feedtype, named after the historic Hochelaga settlement that preceded the city.
description: Named after the indigenous settlement that predates Montreal, this display serif carries a sense of place through its construction. Feedtype have positioned it for headline and editorial contexts where a distinctive serif voice is needed. The naming and framing suggest a typeface rooted in Québécois cultural identity.
foundry_blurb: Feedtype present Hochelaga as a display typeface named after the historic settlement of Hochelaga, the site that would become Montreal, suggesting a connection to the region's deep cultural and historical roots.

name: Wigrum
classification_notes: Wigrum appears to be a grotesque or neo-grotesque sans-serif, consistent with the genre of workhorse display and text faces common to contemporary Canadian type design.
summary: A grotesque sans-serif from Montreal-based Feedtype, suited to both text and display settings.
description: Released by Feedtype, a Canadian type foundry based in Montreal, Wigrum is a grotesque sans-serif designed for versatility across text and display applications. The family is modest in its current scope, offering Regular and Bold weights without italic companions. Its restrained construction places it in the tradition of functional grotesques rather than expressive display work.
foundry_blurb: Feedtype present Wigrum as a typeface available through their Montreal-based foundry, offered as part of their library of contemporary type designs.

name: Vells Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface, as indicated directly by the 'Mono' suffix in the name and its classification as a fixed-width design intended for code or technical use.
summary: A monospaced typeface from Canadian foundry Feedtype, designed for technical and coding applications.
description: Vells Mono is a fixed-width typeface from Feedtype, the Canadian type foundry. As part of the Vells family, it carries a monospaced structure suited to code editors, terminal interfaces and technical documentation. Details on weights and language support were not available from the page content provided.
foundry_blurb: Feedtype present Vells Mono as part of their typeface catalogue, available through their Canadian type foundry platform.

name: More Gothic
classification_notes: The name and page context indicate a grotesque sans-serif in the tradition of 19th-century wood type and gothic lettering, consistent with the broader grotesque category.
summary: A grotesque sans from Canadian foundry Feedtype, drawing on the tradition of 19th-century gothic lettering.
description: More Gothic sits within the grotesque sans tradition, referencing the bold, legible letterforms associated with early American and Canadian wood type. The family appears to offer a restrained set of styles suited to display and editorial applications. As a product of Feedtype, it reflects the foundry's focus on type rooted in North American graphic history.
foundry_blurb: More Gothic is a grotesque typeface available from Feedtype, a Canadian type foundry.

name: Atelier Mono
classification_notes: Designed specifically as a monospaced font, where every character occupies an equal fixed width, suited to a branding context requiring structural consistency and alignment.
summary: A custom monospaced typeface designed for Atelier Général, a Montreal architecture agency, where fixed-width uniformity serves the identity rather than ornamenting it.
description: Commissioned by Feedtype for Atelier Général, the font was built to function as the foundational unit of the agency's visual identity. Its fixed-width structure lends a steady, grid-like quality appropriate to an architectural practice. The design keeps itself deliberately unobtrusive, stepping back to let the architecture work take precedence.
foundry_blurb: Feedtype describe Atelier Mono as a custom monospaced font built to serve as the basic unit for the branding of Atelier Général, a Montreal architecture agency founded by Alexis Naylor and Stéphanie Plourde. They characterise it as functional, reliable and steady, designed to align itself with the projects it is meant to showcase.

name: Ottomono
classification_notes: Ottomono is a fixed-width typeface built from simple, sturdy geometric forms, making it a geometric monospaced design with industrial character.
summary: A fixed-width typeface built from simple geometric shapes, originally commissioned for Ottopapax's cardboard product line Pliage №1.
description: Developed as a custom typeface for Ottopapax, a collective of designers working at the intersection of object and graphic design, Ottomono draws its character from the same logic as the cardboard product it accompanies: sturdy, simple forms produced by mechanical processes. The letterforms carry an industrial directness while remaining legible and familiar. Released through Feedtype, it sits comfortably in contexts where a no-nonsense monospaced voice is needed.
foundry_blurb: Ottomono was developed by Feedtype for Ottopapax, a collaborative project between designers Patrick Bisson, Étienne Hotte and Anouk Pennel. Their Pliage №1 project combined object and graphic design on a single sheet of cardboard shaped by mechanical processes, and Ottomono mirrors that approach through sturdy, simple shapes that feel both industrial and familiar.

name: Uno
classification_notes: Uno's letterforms are directly derived from stencils used on architectural blueprints, giving it a utilitarian, display character suited to exhibition and editorial contexts rather than continuous text.
summary: A stencil display typeface drawn from architectural blueprint lettering, created for a CCA exhibition on modernist urbanism in Casablanca and Chandigarh.
description: Developed for the Canadian Centre for Architecture's exhibition on postcolonial urban planning experiments in Casablanca and Chandigarh, the typeface takes its forms directly from the stencils used on architectural blueprints of that era. The result is a typeface with a distinctly technical, mid-century character that ties its visual language to the subject matter it was made to serve. Its application is best suited to display and exhibition contexts where that utilitarian origin can carry meaning.
foundry_blurb: Feedtype describe Uno as a hydra-headed typeface developed for the CCA exhibition examining how architects, experts, politicians, international agencies and citizens negotiated modern planning in Casablanca and Chandigarh. The exhibition offered a new reading of modern urbanism focused on major experiments in southern countries during the decolonisation wave of the 1950s, and Uno's character shapes are directly inspired by the stencils formerly used for architectural blueprints.
